md: On the unit circle, every angle $\theta$ gives you a point $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. As $\theta$ rotates, that point sweeps around the circle. But what if we *unroll* that motion into a graph ‚Äî plotting $y = \sin\theta$ on regular x-y axes? You get one of the most iconic curves in all of mathematics: the **sine wave**.

There are six trig functions, and each has its own graph. Three of them ‚Äî sine, cosine, tangent ‚Äî are the originals. The other three (cotangent, secant, cosecant) are reciprocals of the originals. Once you understand the first three graphs, the others follow.
## $y = \sin x$
### Linking the circle to the wave

The widget below has two parts: the unit circle on the left, and the graph of $y = \sin\theta$ on the right. As you change $\theta$, both update together. The y-coordinate of the point on the circle is exactly the height of the wave at that angle.

Try sweeping $\theta$ from $0¬∞$ to $360¬∞$. The wave traces out one full cycle: starts at $0$, peaks at $1$ (when $\theta = 90¬∞$), back to $0$ (at $180¬∞$), bottoms at $-1$ (at $270¬∞$), and back to $0$ (at $360¬∞$).
### Period and amplitude
Two numbers describe the shape of a sinusoidal wave:
**Period** ‚Äî the horizontal length of one full cycle. For $y = \sin x$, the period is **$2\pi$** (i.e., $360¬∞$). After that, the pattern repeats forever.
**Amplitude** ‚Äî the vertical 'height' from the middle to the peak. For $y = \sin x$, the amplitude is **$1$**, because $\sin x$ never goes above $1$ or below $-1$.

blocks.title: Why is it periodic?
blocks.body: Adding $2\pi$ (a full revolution) puts you back at the same point on the circle. So $\sin(x + 2\pi) = \sin x$. The wave repeats because the circle does.

md: ## $y = \cos x$

Switch the toggle in the widget above to **cos Œ∏**. You'll see the same shape ‚Äî but shifted to the **left** by $\pi/2$ (i.e., $90¬∞$). Cosine starts at $1$ (when $x = 0$), drops to $0$ (at $\pi/2$), bottoms at $-1$ (at $\pi$), back to $0$ (at $3\pi/2$), and back to $1$ (at $2\pi$).
The cosine curve *is* the sine curve, just shifted. Same shape, different starting point. They're said to be **out of phase** by $\pi/2$ ($90¬∞$).
### Key values to remember
**$\sin x$ is $0$** at $x = 0, \pi, 2\pi, \ldots$
**$\sin x$ is $1$** at $x = \pi/2$ (max).
**$\sin x$ is $-1$** at $x = 3\pi/2$ (min).
**$\cos x$ is $0$** at $x = \pi/2, 3\pi/2, \ldots$
**$\cos x$ is $1$** at $x = 0, 2\pi, \ldots$ (max).
**$\cos x$ is $-1$** at $x = \pi$ (min).
## $y = \tan x$
Tangent is **fundamentally different** from sine and cosine. Because $\tan x = \sin x / \cos x$, it blows up to infinity wherever $\cos x = 0$.

Pick the **tan** tab in the widget below to see it. Notice the dashed red **vertical asymptotes** at $x = \pm\pi/2, \pm 3\pi/2, \ldots$ ‚Äî the function approaches $+\infty$ from one side and $-\infty$ from the other.
**Period of tan:** $\pi$ (not $2\pi$). The full pattern repeats every $\pi$.
**No amplitude** ‚Äî $\tan x$ has no max or min. It goes to $\pm\infty$.
## $y = \cot x$
Cotangent is the reciprocal of tangent: $\cot x = 1/\tan x = \cos x / \sin x$.
Asymptotes happen wherever $\sin x = 0$: at $x = 0, \pm\pi, \pm 2\pi, \ldots$

Like tangent, $\cot x$ has period $\pi$ and no amplitude. But while tan goes from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ on each cycle, cot goes from $+\infty$ to $-\infty$. Click **cot** in the widget above to compare.
## $y = \sec x$ and $y = \csc x$

**Secant:** $\sec x = 1/\cos x$. Asymptotes wherever $\cos x = 0$: at $\pm\pi/2, \pm 3\pi/2, \ldots$ Between asymptotes, sec looks like a U-shape (right-side-up or upside-down). The minimum of each U has $|\sec x| = 1$ ‚Äî so sec **never has values strictly between -1 and 1**.
**Cosecant:** $\csc x = 1/\sin x$. Same idea, asymptotes wherever $\sin x = 0$: at $0, \pm\pi, \pm 2\pi, \ldots$ Csc also stays outside $[-1, 1]$.
Both have period $2\pi$. Try the **sec** and **csc** tabs in the widget above.

blocks.title: Quick reference: periods
blocks.body: **$2\pi$:** $\sin x$, $\cos x$, $\sec x$, $\csc x$. **$\pi$:** $\tan x$, $\cot x$. The reciprocal functions inherit the period of their parent.

md: ## Transformations of trig functions
Once you know one base curve, you get *all* its transformed versions for free. Every trig function we'll write looks like:
Each parameter has a clean geometric meaning:
**$a$** ‚Äî vertical stretch / **amplitude** (for sin and cos). $a = 2$ doubles the peak; $a = -1$ flips the curve upside-down.
**$b$** ‚Äî horizontal stretch / **frequency**. The new period is $\dfrac{2\pi}{b}$ for sin/cos/sec/csc, or $\dfrac{\pi}{b}$ for tan/cot. Bigger $b$ ‚Üí squeezed tighter (shorter period).
**$c$** ‚Äî **phase shift** (horizontal translation). $c > 0$ shifts the graph to the **right**; $c < 0$ shifts left.
**$d$** ‚Äî **vertical shift**. Moves the whole graph up ($d > 0$) or down ($d < 0$). The 'midline' moves to $y = d$.

blocks.title: Heads up: phase shift sign
blocks.body: It's $f(b(x - c))$, with a *minus*. So if you see $y = \sin(2x - \pi)$, factor it: $\sin(2(x - \pi/2))$. Phase shift is $\pi/2$ to the **right**, not $\pi$.

md: ### Play with the transformations
The widget below lets you pick a base function and adjust all four parameters. Watch the equation update at the top, and notice how the period and phase shift readouts respond.
**Things to try:**
Set $a = 2$, $b = 1$, $c = 0$, $d = 0$ ‚Äî sine wave doubled in height.
Set $a = 1$, $b = 2$ ‚Äî period halved (now $\pi$ instead of $2\pi$).
Set $c = \pi/2 \approx 1.57$ ‚Äî sine wave shifted right by a quarter period (now looks like negative cosine, then continue shifting!).
Set $d = 1$ ‚Äî wave's midline rises to $y = 1$.
### Worked example: graph $y = 3\sin(2x) - 1$
Read off the parameters: $a = 3$, $b = 2$, $c = 0$, $d = -1$.
**Amplitude:** $|a| = 3$. The wave swings from $-1 \pm 3$ ‚Äî i.e., from $-4$ (min) to $2$ (max).
**Period:** $\dfrac{2\pi}{b} = \dfrac{2\pi}{2} = \pi$. One full cycle every $\pi$ units (instead of $2\pi$).
**Phase shift:** $0$ ‚Äî no horizontal shift.
**Vertical shift:** $-1$ ‚Äî the midline moves down to $y = -1$.
## Determining the function from a graph
Reverse the process: given a transformed sin/cos curve, read off $a, b, c, d$ from features of the picture.
**Step 1 ‚Äî find the midline.** The horizontal line halfway between the max and min. That's $y = d$. So:
$$d = \dfrac{\max + \min}{2}$$
**Step 2 ‚Äî find the amplitude.** Half the vertical distance from min to max:
$$a = \dfrac{\max - \min}{2}$$
(Use a positive $a$ for the un-flipped curve, negative if the graph is upside-down compared to standard.)
**Step 3 ‚Äî find the period.** Measure the horizontal distance for one complete cycle (e.g., peak to next peak). Then $b = \dfrac{2\pi}{\text{period}}$ (or $\dfrac{\pi}{\text{period}}$ for tan/cot).
**Step 4 ‚Äî find the phase shift.** Pick a known feature of the base curve (e.g., 'sine starts at $0$ going up'). Where does that feature appear on the graph? That x-value is $c$.
### Worked example: identify the function
*A sinusoidal graph has max $5$, min $-1$, period $\pi$, and reaches its first max at $x = \pi/4$. Find the equation as a sine function.*
**Period:** $\pi$, so $b = 2\pi / \pi = 2$.

**Phase shift:** Sine reaches its first max at $x = \pi/2$ in the standard form. Here it's at $\pi/4$. So the curve is shifted left by $\pi/4$ ‚Äî but careful: 'shifted left by $\pi/4$' means $c = -\pi/4$? No: the transformed first max occurs when $b(x - c) = \pi/2$, so $x = c + \pi/(2b) = c + \pi/4$. For this to equal $\pi/4$: $c = 0$.
**Final answer:** $y = 3\sin(2x) + 2$.

blocks.title: Why this matters
blocks.body: Sinusoidal curves describe everything that oscillates: pendulums, sound waves, light, alternating current, tides, even simple harmonic motion in physics. The math you're learning here drives a *lot* of how the real world works.

md: ## Modeling Real-World Phenomena with Sinusoids
Anything that **oscillates regularly** ‚Äî repeats with a steady period ‚Äî can be modeled by a sinusoidal function. The transformation parameters $a, b, c, d$ encode the physics:
**Amplitude $a$** ‚Äî the range of swing (half the distance from min to max).
**Period $\dfrac{2\pi}{b}$** ‚Äî how long one full cycle takes.
**Phase shift $c$** ‚Äî when the cycle starts (offset from $t = 0$).
**Vertical shift $d$** ‚Äî the average / equilibrium / midline value.
Three classic contexts.
### Ferris Wheel Problems
A point on a Ferris wheel traces a **circle**. Plot its **height above the ground** vs. time and you get a sinusoidal curve.
**Identify the parameters:**
**Amplitude $a$** = radius of the wheel (how far up/down from center).
**Vertical shift $d$** = height of the wheel's center above the ground.
**Period $T$** = time for one full revolution. $b = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$.
**Phase shift $c$** = depends on where the rider starts (top, bottom, side).

blocks.title: Choosing sin or cos
blocks.body: Use **cos** when the rider starts at a **max or min** at $t = 0$. Use **sin** when they start at the **midline** going up or down. Either form works ‚Äî they're just shifted versions of each other.

md: **Worked example.** A Ferris wheel has radius $20$ m, with its center $25$ m above the ground. It completes one revolution every $40$ seconds. Riders board at the lowest point. Write the height $h(t)$ as a function of time.
**Setup.** $a = 20$ (radius). $d = 25$ (center height). Period $= 40$ ‚Üí $b = 2\pi / 40 = \pi/20$.
At $t = 0$, height is the **minimum** ($25 - 20 = 5$ m). The minimum of $-\cos$ is at $t = 0$ (since $-\cos 0 = -1$, the lowest). So use:
$$h(t) = -20\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{20}\,t\right) + 25$$

The widget plots $h(t)$ for $80$ seconds (two full revolutions). Bottom = $5$ m, top = $45$ m, period = $40$ s. (Slider controls are hidden here so the model parameters stay fixed; play with the sliders elsewhere in the lesson to see how each one affects the shape.)
### Tides and Water Depth
Tides rise and fall in a near-perfect sinusoid. The lunar cycle drives a period of about **$12.4$ hours** (one cycle from high tide ‚Üí low tide ‚Üí next high tide).
**Identify the parameters:**
**Vertical shift $d$** = mean (average) water depth.
**Amplitude $a$** = half the **tidal range** = $(\text{max depth} - \text{min depth}) / 2$.
**Period $T$** ‚âà $12.4$ h (semi-diurnal) or $\sim 24.8$ h (diurnal).
**Phase shift $c$** = lined up so the model peaks at high tide.
**Worked example.** At a harbor, high tide is $8$ m at $3{:}00$ AM and the next low tide is $2$ m at $9{:}00$ AM. Model depth $D(t)$ where $t$ is hours after midnight.
**Setup.** Mean depth $d = (8 + 2)/2 = 5$ m. Amplitude $a = (8 - 2)/2 = 3$ m. The cycle from high to low takes $9 - 3 = 6$ h, so the **full** period is $T = 12$ h, and $b = 2\pi / 12 = \pi/6$.
High tide is at $t = 3$. Cosine peaks at $0$ in standard form, so we shift right by $3$:
$$D(t) = 3\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}(t - 3)\right) + 5$$
Check: $D(3) = 3 \cdot 1 + 5 = 8$ m (high tide). $D(9) = 3\cos(\pi) + 5 = -3 + 5 = 2$ m (low tide).
Plotted for $24$ hours. You see two full tide cycles, peaks at $3{:}00$ and $15{:}00$ ($3$ PM).
### Spring Motion (Simple Harmonic Motion)
Pull a mass on a spring down from equilibrium and let go ‚Äî it bounces up and down with **simple harmonic motion (SHM)**. The position vs. time is a perfect sinusoid.
**Identify the parameters:**
**Equilibrium = midline $d$** (often $d = 0$ if measured from rest position).
**Amplitude $a$** = how far the mass was pulled from equilibrium.
**Period $T$** = $2\pi \sqrt{m/k}$ for a spring of stiffness $k$ holding mass $m$ (Hooke's law). $b = 2\pi/T$.
**Phase shift $c$** = depends on where motion starts and which direction.
**Worked example.** A mass is pulled $5$ cm below equilibrium and released. It oscillates with period $2$ s. Find position $x(t)$ measured upward from equilibrium.
At $t = 0$, the mass is at its **minimum** position ($x = -5$). For $-\cos$, the min at $t = 0$ matches: $-5\cos(0) = -5$.
Check: $x(0) = -5$ (released from below). $x(1) = -5\cos(\pi) = +5$ (half period later, $5$ cm above equilibrium). $x(2) = -5\cos(2\pi) = -5$ (back to start).
Position vs time for $6$ seconds (three full cycles).

blocks.title: The general modeling recipe
blocks.body: For *any* periodic phenomenon: (1) Find the **midline** = (max + min)/2 ‚Üí $d$. (2) Find the **amplitude** = (max ‚àí min)/2 ‚Üí $a$. (3) Find the **period** $T$ ‚Üí $b = 2\pi/T$. (4) Find a known reference time (peak, trough, midline crossing) ‚Üí $c$. (5) Plug into $y = a\sin(b(t - c)) + d$ or $a\cos(b(t - c)) + d$.
